did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning, specifically Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) including EfficientNetV2, AlexNet, InceptionV3, ResNet50, VGG16, and VGG19, to develop a two-stage classification system for the detection and classification of skin cancer (healthy, benign, malignant, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Melanoma, and Nevus) using images from the ISIC 2019 Challenge and SFA Database.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and deployment of an Android-based mobile application for skin cancer detection. It details a specific methodology using deep learning models (EfficientNetV2, AlexNet, etc.) on datasets (ISIC 2019, SFA Database), reports new results (90% and 91% accuracy), and compares performance against other models, which characterizes it as a technical study producing new results rather than a review.

title: An Efficient Mobile Application for Real-time Detection and Classification of Skin Cancer
journal: 2023 IEEE EMBS Special Topic Conference on Data Science and Engineering in Healthcare, Medicine and Biology
brief_summary: This study developed an efficient Android-based mobile application for the real-time detection and classification of skin cancer to support early diagnosis in primary healthcare. The system employs a two-stage deep learning classification approach using Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), specifically comparing AlexNet, InceptionV3, ResNet50, VGG16, VGG19, and EfficientNetV2. Training data was sourced from the ISIC 2019 Challenge for malignant and benign lesions, supplemented by the SFA Database to include healthy skin images. The primary model classifies lesions as healthy, benign, or malignant, while a secondary model further categorizes malignant cases into Basal Cell Carcinoma, Melanoma, or Nevus. Evaluation results demonstrated that EfficientNetV2 achieved the highest accuracy of 90% and 91% for the major and minor stages, respectively, outperforming other architectures. The study concludes that EfficientNetV2 is the most feasible model for deployment on mobile devices, maintaining performance without deterioration during offline application use.

lesion_types_studied.details: Classified in the minor model (two-stage classification system) as one of three primary categories (healthy, benign, malignant). Added from the SFA Database to differentiate cancerous from healthy skin.
Classified in the minor model as one of three primary categories. Part of the ISIC 2019 Challenge dataset used for the minor model.
Classified in the minor model as one of three primary categories. Further classified into specific types in the major model.
Classified in the major model (secondary stage) as one of three specific malignant skin cancer types. Part of the ISIC 2019 Challenge dataset used for the major model.

Classified in the major model as one of three specific malignant skin cancer types. Identified as one of the most common skin cancer malignancies. Part of the ISIC 2019 Challenge dataset used for the major model.

Classified in the major model as one of three specific malignant skin cancer types (note: paper groups it under malignant classification in the major model context, though clinically often benign, the paper lists it alongside BCC and Melanoma for the major model). Part of the ISIC 2019 Challenge dataset used for the major model.

all_task_types.target: Discrete diagnostic categories: healthy, benign, malignant skin lesions (minor model); Basal Cell Carcinoma, Melanoma, or Nevus (major model).
all_task_types.details: The paper describes a two-stage classification system using CNNs. The minor model classifies images into three classes: healthy, benign, and malignant. The major model classifies malignant lesions into three specific types: Basal Cell Carcinoma, Melanoma, and Nevus. Performance is measured by accuracy (90% and 91%) on these discrete categories.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2019 Challenge Dataset
datasets_used.description: A public dataset from the ISIC 2019 Challenge used for training, validation, and testing. It was used to construct both the minor model (benign vs. malignant) and the major model (specific cancer types).

use.training: Used to train the CNN architectures for both the minor and major models.
use.validation: Used for validation during the construction of the minor and major models.
use.testing: Used for testing the performance of the models (e.g., EfficientNetV2 achieved 90% test accuracy).

datasets_used.name: SFA Database
datasets_used.description: A dataset consisting of images of humans' faces, acquired to provide healthy skin samples. Images were manually cropped to focus on healthy skin features.

use.training: Added as a third class (healthy skin) to the minor model's training dataset to differentiate between cancerous and healthy skin.
use.other: Used to fill the research gap of differentiating between cancerous and healthy skin images.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy and loss metrics, and models were compared based on these descriptive statistics without statistical significance testing.

ISIC 2019 Challenge Dataset (Minor Model.notes: EfficientNetV2 achieved the highest accuracy for the minor stage classification. The performance did not deteriorate after deployment on the Android mobile application, unlike other models.

ISIC 2019 Challenge Dataset (Major Model.notes: EfficientNetV2 outperformed other pre-trained CNN models with 90% test accuracy. Table I lists test accuracy and loss for all compared models.

Comparison with State-of-the-Art Methods.notes: Table II compares the developed EfficientNetV2 model with existing literature. Note that while DCNN [1] reported 95% accuracy, it deteriorated significantly when converted to TfLite and deployed, whereas EfficientNetV2 maintained performance.

architecture_used.main_model: EfficientNetV2 (selected as the best-performing model for deployment)
architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained CNN models were used (AlexNet, InceptionV3, ResNet50, VGG16, VGG19, and EfficientNetV2). Specific pretraining datasets (e.g., ImageNet) are not explicitly stated, but they are referred to as 'pre-trained CNN models'.

architecture_details.system_structure: Two-stage classification system. Stage 1 (Minor Model) classifies healthy, benign, and malignant. Stage 2 (Major Model) classifies malignant lesions into Basal Cell Carcinoma, Melanoma, or Nevus.
architecture_details.deployment_format: TfLite (TensorFlow Lite) for Android mobile application

datasets: ISIC 2019 Challenge Dataset (Minor Model: 4377 images; Major Model: 10,000 images)
SFA Database (Healthy skin images added to Minor Model)

preprocessing_techniques: Manual cropping of healthy skin images from the SFA Database to focus on healthy skin features
conclusions: The study developed an Android-based mobile application for the real-time detection and classification of skin cancer to support early diagnosis in primary healthcare.

A two-stage deep learning classification system was implemented: a minor model classifies images as healthy, benign, or malignant, and a major model further classifies malignant lesions into Basal Cell Carcinoma, Melanoma, or Nevus.

EfficientNetV2 was identified as the best-performing model, achieving 91% accuracy for the minor stage (healthy, benign, malignant) and 90% test accuracy for the major stage (Basal Cell Carcinoma, Melanoma, Nevus).
EfficientNetV2 outperformed other tested CNN architectures (AlexNet, InceptionV3, ResNet50, VGG16, VGG19) in terms of accuracy and feasibility for mobile deployment.

Unlike other models such as the DCNN from literature which achieved 95% accuracy in training but suffered significant performance deterioration when converted to TfLite and deployed on mobile, EfficientNetV2 maintained its performance after deployment.
The application accepts both real-time camera images and clinical images as input, aiming to improve survival rates by enabling users to monitor suspected lesions at an early stage.
important_remarks: The study lacks external validation; the model was trained and tested exclusively on the ISIC 2019 Challenge dataset and the SFA Database, limiting generalizability to other populations or imaging devices.

The 'healthy skin' class was constructed by manually cropping facial skin images from the SFA Database, introducing potential dataset bias where the model may associate facial skin texture/features with health rather than general skin health.
No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was reported; performance comparisons between models are based solely on descriptive accuracy metrics.
Key training hyperparameters (optimizer, learning rate, loss function, number of epochs) are not explicitly reported, hindering reproducibility.

The paper highlights a critical deployment caveat: while other models (e.g., DCNN from literature) achieved high accuracy in training, they suffered significant performance deterioration when converted to TensorFlow Lite (TfLite) for mobile deployment, whereas EfficientNetV2 maintained its performance.

The application is designed for real-time detection using both camera and clinical images, but the clinical utility is limited by the absence of ground-truth histopathological verification for the mobile app's inputs in this study.
The study is a conference paper with limited scope, focusing primarily on model efficiency and mobile deployment feasibility rather than comprehensive clinical validation or user study outcomes.
